CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-29-2025 Continued To Aug-27-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEFENDANT MOHAMMED AZAZ'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar May-12-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar May-12-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MOHAMMED AZAZ's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Azaz's motion to quash is granted.

Plaintiff has the evidentiary burden to demonstrate proper service to warrant this court's exercise of jurisdiction. (Dill v. Berquist Construction Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1426, 1440 ["[T]he burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service"].) "[I]n California, the original service of process, which confers jurisdiction, must conform to statutory requirements or all that follows is void." (Ruttenberg v. Ruttenberg (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 801, 809 [internal quotation marks and citation omitted].) Plaintiff relies on Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, for the argument that the service statutes should be liberally construed if the defendant has received actual notice. But as Summers makes clear, actual notice alone is not enough; a service statute may be liberally construed but it may not be set aside, regardless of whether there is actual notice. (Id at p. 415 ["no California appellate court has gone so far as to uphold a service of process solely on the ground the defendant received actual notice when there has been a complete failure to comply with the statutory requirements for service."].)

The court grants plaintiff's unopposed request for judicial notice of this court's records per Evidence Code section 452(d).

Plaintiff contends that plaintiff substitute-served defendant at 6277 Jericho Turnpike, Commack, NY. (CCP 415.20(b).) The claimed service was defective. The only competent evidence demonstrates that the address is not defendant's dwelling house, usual place of abode, usual place of business, or usual mailing address. (Azaz Decl., pars. 4-6.) Defendant has personal knowledge regarding his domicile and place of work. At most, plaintiff has shown that the property may be affiliated with defendant's family (or defendant's father who shares the same name), but there is no competent evidence to support a finding of proper substitute service and defendant's evidentiary objections have merit. In addition, plaintiff's declaration of diligence is lacking since plaintiff's process server only went to the Commack address once before leaving the papers with Lynn "Doe." (Defendant's RJN, Ex. C.) In sum, plaintiff has not met plaintiff's burden of proof.

The court denies the request for a continuance so that plaintiff may conduct jurisdictional discovery. Generally a continuance for jurisdictional discovery may be had where a plaintiff demonstrates "that discovery is likely to lead to the production of evidence of facts establishing jurisdiction." (In re Automobile Antitrust Cases I & II (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 100, 127.) But service of process itself is what confers power on the court to compel defendant to respond to discovery. Because service was defective here, the court lacks that power. Moreover, plaintiff's request for jurisdictional discovery does not satisfy the standard of showing that discovery is likely to lead to the production of evidence establishing that substitute service was properly made, which is what is necessary here to establish jurisdiction. Even if plaintiff could establish through discovery that defendant resides at or works at the Commack address, contrary to the Azaz declaration, that evidence would not cure plaintiff's process server's failure to provide an effective declaration of diligence.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
